author: @rickibaron4816
text: It is nice to see you getting into the CNC machines.  I am a DIY type of person and have built, from the ground up, 2 CNC machines.  What I find the trickiest thing to figure out is three things.  1. The rate at which your CNC feeds the cutter into your part.  This changes  depending on the material that you are cutting.  2. The motor speed that is spinning the cutter.  3. The type of cutter you are using.  I started using HSS, then switched to Carbide bits.  Of course the number of flutes that your bit has along with the angle the flutes are made make a difference as well.  I have found that a single flute carbide ONSRUD bit works very well with plastics.  I prefer 3 flute carbide bits for Aluminum.  With Aluminum your have to slow down the feed speed at witch you are cutting.  Also cutting metal you have to watch out that the cutter does not get metal gummed on to the cutter.  Usually, metals need coolant to cut properly.  As chip removal can be critical for almost all materials being cut. With plastic your can feed rate can be a little faster.  With Wood you the feed rate set as fast as your cutter can go.  Otherwise you will burn the wood if it is too slow. You may have to experiment a bit to figure out all these things on your machine.  I would suggest buying several cutters so that i you break one it can then be replaced.  If a cutter brakes then usually the feed rate is set too high.  I hope that this will help you a little.  Your friend Ricki.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: speaking of which , I really miss the chemistry experiment videos you used to do more frequently :)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
on your PCB making, if you plan on chemical etching for the copper, you would want to drill the holes first using the mill then use a clear method you can see the holes through while applying your acid resist coating, which I found vinyl sticker stock works perfectly and the acid I used was vinegar and peroxide mixture, :) (so much cleaner than the standard expected ferric chloride, really wish I had known back when I was making things using acid etching ages ago,) and hte peroxide I used that works so well was just standard pharmacy grade 3% peroxide, nothing crazy like the 30% used in hair salons for bleaching hair out or anything, The good thing too on that I am finding (from making the mistake of etching first) is that I can hang the boards in a plastic container in the vinegar and peroxide solution with both sides of a double sided circuit board and etch it all at once, and the simple action of gravity is enough to drag down and keep a clean surface for the acid peroxide mix to get to the copper under it, 

My next trick is going to be testing out the copper acetate with fresh peroxide to do aluminum etching :) (have done it a little on a thermal blanket but had it laying down in a pyrex dish which you dont even need for this, plastic is fine and no need for  heating so going to see if I can do a bit better job this time (the mylar in those blankets has one heck of a dielectric constant for as thin as it is, like 400v per layer so you can get in the 10s if not 100s of thousands of volts of capability in a simple mm thick layer stack of them never mind the options for using it in battery making)

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: on array of tools you have now the two you need to make metal machinery as the lathe makes the parts that need smooth circular cuts like spindles and bearings, and the mill will make the body to hold those parts as they spin to make all sorts of stuff XD

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: one other thing, if you are getting carbide bits and the shanks are solid carbide as those smaller ones tend to be,, you might look into some diamond grinding tools to turn broken bits into some incredible hand carving tools that can cut through glass and hard steels with great ease.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
oh that waxy stuff or even just plain wax on the bit can keep plastic from getting quite so hot though it seems that running the slowest speed or having dwelling times programmed into it so it has time to cool back down with that air assist so you dont have the plastic bind on the bit and break it. :) Additionally I am guessing you will start realizing you can tell if your machine is having trouble or wizing along just fine, based on the noise it makes as it is cutting parts :) (ie the more clean and running well it is as it cuts the quieter it is, as the noise is an indication of excessive energy imparted into bits and materials you are cutting caused by inefficiency of the cutting action so you want it to be as quiet at the end of use as it was when you started up :) 

Machinery's handbook seems to be quite a good source for how to know what speeds things can handle and where the sweet spot of getting speed and longevity is actually at, where going slower or faster will both shorten the life of the cutter, :) 

Cant wait to see you take it to the finishing processes that can be had with some of those bits at the last machine shop machinery expo that you can see here on youtube. (like mirror finishing from a mill, just crazy good work.)

author: @Alexander-uj5pb
text: Always interesting💯💯👌👌

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
would be good to try out tips and tricks for it and let people know what your results are for this, like using something like the waxy stuff found on Rio Grande (a jewelery manufacturing supply company that I would drop the link but I know that would block the comment sadly) they have this blue stick of waxy stuff that you apply to bits and burrs called burr ease or something like that, that ends chattering and keeps the bit cool, 

additionally though is the use of something such as that applying it periodically to keep stuff like aluminum from bonding back to the metal bit and binding it up as aluminum and titanium like to do. (kinda does what your video on how to get metal coating from that steel wool application of the nano particles suspended in a de-oxidizing system like ascorbic acid and such.)  

additionally the parts you made with the mill look fantastic. :)

author: @EUPThatsMe
text: I got a cheap Desktop CNC machine from Amazon (~$400) to do home PCB's but the open frame meant that it spewed fiberglass into the room causing health issues.  I built a box for it but it too leaked fiberglass dust.  I was able to get some FR2 boards (based on resin and paper) but while less dangerous than the fiberglass FR4 it still creates a lot of resin dust, even if you only use the CNC for drilling the holes.  In the future I plan to only use it outdoors.  In the meantime I just did my board the old fashioned way with resist and etching liquid.  Some of the more powerful laser cutters can etch away the copper layer on PCBs w/o damaging the underlying base.  These also have much better airflow for exhausting the copper vapor outdoors.  While I've yet to try it, some have suggested using a resist pen in the CNC to draw the pattern for standard liquid etching.  In all those cases the through holes & vias have to have small copper inserts to conduct from the front to the back of the board.

replies.author: @monsaka7827
replies.text: A box with a HEPA vacuum should solve that.

replies.author: @EUPThatsMe
replies.text: ​@monsaka7827I have considered putting it in a paint box

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is one of the issues with subtractive methods mate - but I think you have good idea in your mind - a paint box should do it

author: @ivolol
text:
If you made the enclosure plates in metal, would they have been easier to create entirely using the CNC? 

I always worry about an online (online here, in the sense that you download and update it from an online app store on a compatible device) app being used for control, because will the software still be supported & running in 10 years time when what is called "Android" is now something possibly entirely evolved? Will the machine be still easily controllable if in a decade's time, the worst had happened and Makera had went under as a going business and stopped updating anything? For you personally you'll probably keep that specific tablet with the machine for years, but what's the situation for someone who bought one of these second hand?

I particularly like the remark, "If you're not making mistakes, you're not learning anything". I'll have to keep that in mind more often.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: About the same as if I had entirely CNC'd the acrylic I think mate cheers
